basic_identity.name: Iris Leung
basic_identity.occupation: Regional commercial planning manager at a consumer healthcare distributor
basic_identity.income_level: Upper-middle income; about HKD 82,000 per month plus a variable annual bonus
basic_identity.family_structure: Lives with her husband, who teaches secondary school, and their six-year-old daughter
basic_identity.housing_status: Owner-occupier in a private flat with an ongoing mortgage

personality_belief.decision_style: Methodical but not slow. She gathers her own inputs first, narrows options quickly, and only asks for human advice when she can already frame the question.
personality_belief.risk_tolerance: Comfortable with normal market volatility and concentrated views in parts of her portfolio, but impatient with opaque structures, suitability theatre, and products that hide downside in soft language.
personality_belief.trust_orientation: Trust is earned through transparency, usable detail, and visible alignment of incentives. Brand helps her open the page, but it does not close the decision.

technology_profile.tech_savviness: High for a mainstream banking customer. She is comfortable linking accounts, exporting data, using broker dashboards, and maintaining her own portfolio spreadsheet.
technology_profile.privacy_concern: Moderately high. She will share data when the utility is concrete and reversible, but she assumes any bank data access can later be used for targeting.
technology_profile.automation_openness: Open to automation for monitoring, alerts, categorisation, and reporting. Resistant to automation that acts on her behalf or quietly narrows her choices.

economic_profile.price_sensitivity: Not bargain-hunting by default, but highly sensitive to paying for something she can approximate herself with effort.
economic_profile.subscription_tolerance: Selective. She tolerates subscriptions for tools that save repeated time or reduce costly mistakes, but dislikes layered fees on top of existing banking relationships.
economic_profile.switching_cost: Moderate. She can move activity between apps and brokers, but she avoids migrating core accounts unless the gain is sustained and obvious.

core_values: Not being talked into products she does not fully understand
fears: Making a preventable mistake because information was fragmented
Paying hidden fees for packaged advice
Being profiled as a sales lead after sharing more data
Finding out too late that her overall portfolio risk is very different from what each account suggests separately
aspirations: Reach a point where her family's long-term investing feels organised rather than manually patched together
Keep enough flexibility to help with future education costs and support both sets of parents if needed
Use bank services when they are genuinely useful, not merely convenient

life_story.career_path: Iris started in a junior analyst role at a distributor after university, moved into commercial reporting, and gradually became the person who translated messy regional sales data into decisions senior managers could act on. Her career is not glamorous, but it made her comfortable with dashboards, assumptions, and the difference between a clean summary and the underlying mess.
life_story.current_daily_routine: She wakes around 6:20, checks overnight US market moves while making coffee, gets her daughter ready for school, and commutes partly by MTR while scanning market headlines. Work is spreadsheet-heavy and meeting-heavy. At lunch she may glance at price alerts or read a few community comments. Evenings are family time, with investing research usually pushed to after 10 p.m. on the sofa with her phone and laptop open together.
frustrations: Her assets are split across bank holdings, broker positions, MPF, and a legacy fund account, so she never sees true exposure in one place without manual work.
Bank investment interfaces often feel simplified in the wrong way: too much reassurance, too little real analysis.
Relationship managers sound helpful until the conversation drifts toward product inventory.
Risk questionnaires and suitability labels often feel disconnected from what she actually holds and why.

behavior_profile.buying_behavior: She researches first, compares independently, reads the unattractive parts of the offer, and often waits a few days unless there is a time-sensitive market reason. She is willing to trial digitally if onboarding is reversible and the first value appears quickly.
decision_blockers: Any sense that the tool exists mainly to generate leads for sales staff
Vague claims like institutional-grade without showing what is actually different
Data-sharing requests that are broader than the stated use case
Fees that stack on top of advisory or custody charges
Outputs that summarise risk without showing drivers, assumptions, or timing of data refresh

active_pain_points: She can evaluate single positions reasonably well, but whole-portfolio risk is harder because accounts are fragmented.
She knows she has overlapping exposures across US tech, broad ETFs, and fund holdings, but quantifying it is tedious.
Currency exposure is easy to ignore until a sharp move makes it visible.
She wants scenario analysis that is more concrete than generic warnings but less institutional-jargon-heavy than professional tools.
jobs_to_be_done: See aggregate portfolio risk across bank and non-bank holdings before making changes
Spot sector, geography, currency, and style concentration she may be underestimating
Understand whether her actual holdings fit her stated risk profile in a useful, not purely compliance, sense
Get plain-language interpretation without surrendering execution control
problem_context.willingness_to_pay: She would pay a modest fee only if the analysis genuinely reduces manual work, includes external holdings, and does not turn into a disguised advisory upsell.

sensitive_reality_layer.fairness_and_inclusion_profile: Iris does not frame herself in activist language, but she notices when financial products quietly assume a stay-at-home spouse, a male decision-maker, or a simple nuclear household with one obvious risk owner. She expects professional neutrality and competent language more than symbolic messaging.
response_boundaries: She will not discuss family planning, fertility, or health matters in a financial product context unless there is a direct and optional reason.
She dislikes being pushed to disclose detailed household relationship dynamics to unlock a tool.
She does not want her investing behaviour translated into personality labels and reused in marketing.

canonical_biography.life_arc_summary: Iris grew up in a disciplined, middle-income Hong Kong household where money was managed carefully but not fearfully. She became professionally fluent in data at work, then gradually applied that same habit to personal finance. A few ordinary but sticky experiences with fees, fragmented accounts, and product-led bank advice pushed her toward self-directed investing rather than away from investing altogether.

decade_timeline.life_context: She grew up in Kwun Tong in a family that was stable, busy, and careful with money rather than expressive about it.
decade_timeline.specific_scene: At about age eight, she sat at a folding table while her mother copied grocery totals and utility payments into a ruled notebook next to passbooks and Octopus top-up receipts.
decade_timeline.product_relevant_memory: She learned that a neat monthly summary can hide many small tradeoffs underneath it.
decade_timeline.social_or_relationship_context: Her parents were cooperative but practical; affection was shown through planning and reliability more than long conversations.
decade_timeline.money_or_effort_tradeoff: The family spent time tracking money to avoid stress later.
decade_timeline.beliefs_formed: Control comes from visibility, and visibility takes effort.
decade_timeline.current_reaction_link: She is attracted to tools that reduce manual tracking, but only if they preserve transparency.

decade_timeline.chapter_title: Skeptical of Tips, Respectful of Homework
decade_timeline.life_context: Secondary school years were academically focused, with long commutes and a household rhythm built around work, school, and careful budgeting.
decade_timeline.specific_scene: Around age fourteen, she watched her father read stock pages over breakfast, circle a few names in the newspaper, then tell her that hearing an idea and understanding it were not the same thing.
decade_timeline.product_relevant_memory: Market conversation existed at home, but it was cautious and never romantic.
decade_timeline.social_or_relationship_context: Advice in the extended family often came with ego attached; her parents taught her to listen politely and decide privately.
decade_timeline.money_or_effort_tradeoff: The family preferred slow, understandable progress over flashy shortcuts.
decade_timeline.beliefs_formed: Do the homework yourself; borrowed conviction is fragile.
decade_timeline.current_reaction_link: She resists bank narratives that feel too clean or too persuasive without enough underlying detail.

decade_timeline.life_context: She entered the workforce, learned to operate inside Excel-heavy commercial roles, and started saving seriously.
decade_timeline.specific_scene: At twenty-seven, during a lunch-hour meeting in a Central branch, she let an RM talk her into a China-focused fund with a polished deck and softer language around charges than she would now tolerate.
decade_timeline.product_relevant_memory: The fund underperformed badly relative to her expectation, and the upfront fee stung more than the market loss because it felt avoidable.
decade_timeline.social_or_relationship_context: Friends were starting to discuss MPF allocation, travel, and early property ambitions; everyone sounded more confident than they really were.
decade_timeline.money_or_effort_tradeoff: She paid for convenience and a sense of legitimacy, then concluded she had outsourced the wrong part of the decision.
decade_timeline.beliefs_formed: Brand and branch comfort do not equal alignment; fees matter most when the outcome is disappointing.
decade_timeline.current_reaction_link: She is open to bank analytics but suspicious of any handoff from insight to sales push.

decade_timeline.chapter_title: Managing Fragmentation While Life Gets Denser
decade_timeline.life_context: Marriage, a mortgage, a child, and more income made her financial life broader rather than simpler.
decade_timeline.specific_scene: On a Sunday night after a sharp market week, she sat with her phone showing broker positions, her laptop showing a spreadsheet, and a bank app showing only partial holdings, trying to estimate how much US tech exposure she really had across direct stocks, ETFs, and a fund.
decade_timeline.product_relevant_memory: She realised her problem was no longer lack of access to markets but lack of coherent portfolio visibility.
decade_timeline.social_or_relationship_context: Her husband is sensible and not deeply engaged in markets, so final investment detail work falls mostly to her.
decade_timeline.money_or_effort_tradeoff: She accepts spending time to stay in control, but resents repeated admin that adds no insight.
decade_timeline.beliefs_formed: A good tool should compress admin, not compress thinking.
decade_timeline.current_reaction_link: A bank app feature that genuinely aggregates risk across holdings could win her attention, but not her trust automatically.

formative_events.event_summary: Watching her mother keep simple but disciplined household records
formative_events.impact: Made visibility feel responsible rather than obsessive

formative_events.event_summary: Absorbing her father's cautious attitude toward market chatter
formative_events.impact: Created a habit of separating ideas from conviction

formative_events.event_summary: Regretting a bank-sold fund purchase with fees she had not interrogated hard enough
formative_events.impact: Lowered trust in RM-led recommendations and sharpened fee sensitivity

formative_events.event_summary: Juggling broker, bank, MPF, and family budgeting at the same time
formative_events.impact: Turned portfolio visibility into a practical problem worth solving

canonical_biography.current_identity: She sees herself as a competent retail investor, not a hobbyist trader and not a passive delegate. She is proud of being able to understand more than summary labels, but she knows her workflow is messier than she wants.
canonical_biography.current_daily_life: Her days are structured, full, and slightly compressed. She fits investing around work and family rather than treating it as a separate identity project.
canonical_biography.product_research_implications: She is ideal for testing whether bank-delivered analytics can overcome distrust of product-led advice, whether external aggregation feels worth the privacy tradeoff, and whether advanced analysis can be translated without being dumbed down.

childhood_environment.family_structure_and_stability: Two-parent household, materially stable but budget-conscious. No dramatic instability, but little waste and little appetite for financial embarrassment.
childhood_environment.caregiver_dynamics: Her mother handled day-to-day cash flow and record-keeping; her father projected calm authority and occasional market curiosity. Decisions were discussed quietly rather than theatrically.
childhood_environment.emotional_climate: Practical, loving, slightly restrained. Problems were solved by preparing, not by openly processing feelings for long.
childhood_environment.money_environment: Money was neither taboo nor abundant. The household treated planning as normal adult behaviour, and avoidable fees were considered foolish.
childhood_environment.authority_and_rules: Rules were clear and usually justified through consequence rather than threat. Adults expected self-discipline more than performance.

ordinary_childhood_scenes.setting: Kitchen table in a Kwun Tong flat
ordinary_childhood_scenes.scene: Her mother balanced receipts, utility bills, and passbooks while Iris sorted coins into small piles and asked why some weeks meant no extra dessert after dim sum.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.lesson_at_the_time: Small spending choices add up, and written records settle arguments faster than memory.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.adult_echo: She still trusts a clean ledger or dashboard more than a reassuring summary.

ordinary_childhood_scenes.scene: Her father scanned finance pages and TV tickers, then shrugged off a relative's stock tip by saying that hearing a name is not the same as owning the risk.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.lesson_at_the_time: People enjoy sounding informed more than being accountable.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.adult_echo: She discounts enthusiasm unless someone can explain downside and timing.

ordinary_childhood_scenes.setting: Family dinner with an aunt who worked in insurance sales
ordinary_childhood_scenes.scene: She watched her mother ask calm, awkward questions about lock-up periods and fees until the room went a bit quiet and the pitch ended politely.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.lesson_at_the_time: You can say no without making a scene if you know what to ask.
ordinary_childhood_scenes.adult_echo: Her objections to financial products are usually polite, pointed, and fee-focused.

adult_decision_links.childhood_pattern: Seeing household money tracked line by line
adult_decision_links.adult_value_or_assumption: Visibility is part of responsibility, not an optional extra
adult_decision_links.product_judgment_effect: She is drawn to tools that reveal the full picture and suspicious of summary-only interfaces
adult_decision_links.limits_of_inference: This does not make her inherently frugal in every category; she will still spend when value feels concrete

adult_decision_links.childhood_pattern: Hearing adults dismiss loose market tips
adult_decision_links.adult_value_or_assumption: Confidence without explanation is weak evidence
adult_decision_links.product_judgment_effect: She challenges vague authority claims like institutional-grade unless they are demonstrated
adult_decision_links.limits_of_inference: She can still be swayed by convenience if the friction gap is large enough

adult_decision_links.childhood_pattern: Watching her mother question sales language without embarrassment
adult_decision_links.adult_value_or_assumption: Asking about fees and conditions is normal, not rude
adult_decision_links.product_judgment_effect: She tests products through pricing clarity and data-use terms very early
adult_decision_links.limits_of_inference: She is less confrontational in work settings than at home, so she may delay objections if the interface is confusing

adult_decision_links.childhood_pattern: Growing up in a household where care was shown through planning
adult_decision_links.adult_value_or_assumption: Competence can be a form of care for family
adult_decision_links.product_judgment_effect: She is more willing to invest effort in financial tools that visibly improve family resilience
adult_decision_links.limits_of_inference: It does not mean she enjoys finance emotionally; some of her diligence is duty rather than excitement

childhood_environment.uncertainty_notes: Her childhood clearly shaped her comfort with records and skepticism toward sales pressure, but it did not mechanically determine her investing style. Professional exposure to dashboards and one bad fund experience also played major roles.

domain_fit.fit_summary: Strong fit for a Hong Kong affluent-retail banking test focused on portfolio analytics. She has meaningful investable assets, fragmented holdings, active market engagement, and a credible reason to compare bank tools against broker tools.
domain_fit.why_she_matters_for_this_study: She can distinguish between shallow reassurance and useful analytics, and her low trust in RM-led pushes makes her a good test of whether institutional-grade framing can genuinely improve wallet share or merely trigger suspicion.
domain_fit.non_fit_risks: She is more analytically engaged than a mass retail novice, so she may underrepresent customers who need very basic education first.

pricing_logic.price_sensitivity_level: Moderate but sharp-edged. She is not allergic to paying; she is allergic to paying twice for the same value.
pricing_logic.personal_payment_comfort: Comfortable with a small monthly fee or an annual premium if it replaces manual consolidation and delivers decision-grade analysis across holdings.
pricing_logic.preferred_pricing_model: Bundled into a premium banking tier she already uses, or a clearly bounded add-on with a trial period and easy cancellation.
pricing_logic.pricing_objection: If I already keep assets with the bank, why am I paying extra just to understand the risk you can already see on your side?
pricing_logic.what_makes_price_feel_fair: Cross-account coverage, timely refreshes, scenario depth, downloadable reports, and visible separation between analytics access and product-selling pressure.

workflow_adoption_model.awareness_to_habit: She notices an in-app prompt, ignores it once, returns during a volatile market period, runs it first on partial bank-held assets, then decides whether the value is high enough to link external accounts. Habit only forms if the output changes at least one real decision within the first few uses.
workflow_adoption_model.trial_conditions: Fast setup, clear permission controls, no RM call forced at the end, and enough example output to judge depth before sharing everything.
workflow_adoption_model.dropout_risk: High if the first report feels generic, stale, or obviously tied to cross-sell recommendations.
workflow_adoption_model.human_support_role: Optional RM interpretation is acceptable only after the self-serve analysis stands on its own.

first_checks: What holdings can it actually include, and how current is the data?
Does it show concentration, currency, and scenario logic in a way that can be inspected?
What exactly happens to my data after I connect external assets?
Does risk-profile mismatch mean genuine portfolio analysis or just suitability paperwork?
positive_signals: Shows overlap across direct stocks, ETFs, funds, and MPF rather than treating each bucket separately
Plain-language explanation with drill-down detail available
Clear methodology notes and refresh timestamps
No immediate push to book a product conversation after viewing results
negative_signals: Any language implying personalised advice before enough context is gathered
Outputs that are colourful but non-actionable
One-click connect flows that feel broader than necessary
Premium pricing attached to a report that looks like a dressed-up risk questionnaire
questions_they_would_ask: If I hold the same names through different vehicles, can you detect overlap properly?
How do you treat MPF and insurance-linked holdings if the data is delayed or incomplete?
Can I edit or exclude positions manually if the import is wrong?
If the report flags a mismatch, what is the basis and what does not happen automatically?
product_reaction_rules.difference_between_curiosity_and_purchas: Curiosity comes from the promise of one-screen portfolio visibility. Purchase or sustained use only comes if the analytics are materially deeper than what she can infer from her existing broker tools and spreadsheet.

identity_and_inclusion_reaction.baseline_expectation: Use neutral, adult language and do not assume one spouse is the serious money person.
identity_and_inclusion_reaction.positive_response: She notices when examples include different family structures or decision styles without making a performance of it.
identity_and_inclusion_reaction.negative_response: She disengages if a financial flow starts making lifestyle assumptions to fake personalisation.
identity_and_inclusion_reaction.importance_to_adoption: Moderate. Inclusion alone does not sell the product, but careless assumptions lower trust quickly.

generic_new_product.reaction_basis: She defaults to pragmatic curiosity if the claimed benefit maps to a recurring annoyance in her life.
generic_new_product.first_question: What exactly becomes easier after the first week of use?
generic_new_product.positive_trigger: A visible before-and-after improvement in time or clarity
generic_new_product.trust_requirement: A concise demo, clear limits, and no need to over-commit upfront
generic_new_product.likely_objection: This sounds tidy in theory, but where does it actually save me effort?
generic_new_product.persona_specific_example: A home-organisation app interests her only if it reduces repeated coordination, not if it mainly adds another dashboard.

ai_product.reaction_basis: She treats AI as useful summarisation and pattern detection, not as an authority source by default.
ai_product.first_question: What part is genuinely analysed versus just reworded for me?
ai_product.positive_trigger: Transparent inputs, editable assumptions, and outputs she can verify elsewhere
ai_product.negative_trigger: Anthropomorphic confidence or hidden data use
ai_product.trust_requirement: Explain where the model helps, where it can be wrong, and what data it touches
ai_product.likely_objection: If I cannot inspect the basis, I cannot tell whether this is intelligence or just packaging.
ai_product.persona_specific_example: She would use AI to summarise annual reports faster, but not to generate unattended trade ideas.

subscription_product.reaction_basis: She compares the subscription against the recurring mental load it removes.
subscription_product.first_question: Will I still want this after the trial novelty fades?
subscription_product.positive_trigger: Repeated utility that becomes part of a routine
subscription_product.negative_trigger: Multiple pricing tiers engineered to confuse
subscription_product.trust_requirement: Simple cancellation and no penalty for downgrading
subscription_product.likely_objection: I do not mind paying; I mind paying indefinitely for something I use only when there is a problem.
subscription_product.persona_specific_example: She keeps one paid market-data tool because it answers a recurring need, but ignores most premium lifestyle apps.

family_or_household_product.reaction_basis: She filters for whether it reduces hidden coordination work at home.
family_or_household_product.first_question: Does this create more upkeep for the person already organising everything?
family_or_household_product.positive_trigger: Shared visibility without constant manual follow-up
family_or_household_product.trust_requirement: Respectful default settings and easy sharing controls
family_or_household_product.likely_objection: Do not sell me convenience that becomes my admin job.
family_or_household_product.persona_specific_example: A family budgeting tool appeals only if her husband can participate without needing her to maintain the whole system.

health_or_wellbeing_product.reaction_basis: She is interested but wary of products that turn ordinary stress into a monetised diagnosis.
health_or_wellbeing_product.first_question: Is this giving me a tool or making me responsible for another metric?
health_or_wellbeing_product.positive_trigger: Concrete, low-drama support that fits a working parent's schedule
health_or_wellbeing_product.trust_requirement: Professional grounding and discreet handling of personal data
health_or_wellbeing_product.likely_objection: I need something usable, not something that makes me feel watched.
health_or_wellbeing_product.persona_specific_example: She would consider a sleep-improvement product if it was practical and private, not community-driven.

financial_product.reaction_basis: She assumes incentives may be mixed and looks for where the sales motive enters.
financial_product.first_question: How does this make money from me if I say yes?
financial_product.positive_trigger: Unbundled pricing, inspectable assumptions, and preserved user control
financial_product.negative_trigger: Advisory language that arrives before evidence
financial_product.trust_requirement: Fee clarity, data-use clarity, and no forced human handoff
financial_product.likely_objection: This may be useful, but I need to know whether I am buying analysis or entering a funnel.
financial_product.persona_specific_example: A bank analytics feature gets a chance; a pre-filled product recommendation carousel right after the report damages trust immediately.

education_or_child_product.reaction_basis: She is open if it supports real learning rather than competitive parenting theatre.
education_or_child_product.first_question: Will this actually help my daughter engage, or just give me another performance metric?
education_or_child_product.positive_trigger: Evidence of practical use and manageable parent effort
education_or_child_product.negative_trigger: Fear-based upselling around a child's future
education_or_child_product.trust_requirement: Age-appropriate design and no guilt-heavy messaging
education_or_child_product.likely_objection: Do not turn aspiration into pressure packaging.
education_or_child_product.persona_specific_example: She pays for a focused English reading programme, but ignores products that mainly sell parental anxiety.

workflow_or_productivity_product.reaction_basis: She values tools that reduce reconciliation work and preserve auditability.
workflow_or_productivity_product.first_question: Can I still see the underlying details when something looks off?
workflow_or_productivity_product.positive_trigger: Fewer repeated steps with better traceability
workflow_or_productivity_product.likely_objection: If the shortcut removes visibility, it is not really a shortcut for me.
workflow_or_productivity_product.persona_specific_example: She adopts planning tools at work only when they make exception-handling easier, not just dashboards prettier.

identity_sensitive_product.reaction_basis: She wants control over what is inferred and who can see it.
identity_sensitive_product.first_question: Why do you need this information, and can I opt out without breaking the product?
identity_sensitive_product.positive_trigger: Specific purpose, limited use, and respectful defaults
identity_sensitive_product.negative_trigger: Broad data capture framed as personalisation
identity_sensitive_product.trust_requirement: Purpose limitation and visible permission controls
identity_sensitive_product.likely_objection: You are asking for more intimacy than the value justifies.
identity_sensitive_product.persona_specific_example: She would decline a finance app that wants detailed family-role profiling to tailor advice.

high_friction_onboarding.reaction_basis: She tolerates friction only if the value is clearly proportional and the sequence feels competent.
high_friction_onboarding.first_question: Why is each step necessary, and what do I get before the final step?
high_friction_onboarding.positive_trigger: Progressive setup with value shown early
high_friction_onboarding.negative_trigger: Front-loaded permissions and identity verification without context
high_friction_onboarding.trust_requirement: Step-level explanations and the option to stop without penalty
high_friction_onboarding.likely_objection: You are asking me to commit before you have proved usefulness.
high_friction_onboarding.persona_specific_example: She might link one external account first if she can preview the output before linking the rest.

primary_interests: Practical travel planning for short family trips to Japan or Taiwan
Comparing household systems that reduce repeated admin
low_energy_hobbies: Scrolling market commentary after her daughter sleeps
Watching apartment organisation videos without always acting on them
Reading restaurant reviews for places she may never get around to trying
social_hobbies: Dinner catch-ups with two university friends who also invest
Occasional parent-group outings tied to school schedules
Talking through market moves with one colleague over lunch
private_hobbies: Keeping a quiet list of purchase regrets and what she learned from them
Browsing investor forum threads mainly to sample sentiment, not to post
aspirational_hobbies: Learning enough Japanese to travel with less dependence on translation apps
Taking a formal course on portfolio construction if time opens up
abandoned_hobbies: A period of frequent baking during the pandemic
Trying to learn options trading beyond a basic level

interest_depth.depth_level: Deep but bounded; it is a serious competence area rather than her entire identity
interest_depth.why_it_matters: It gives her a sense of adult control and future-proofing that feels earned rather than outsourced.
interest_depth.how_it_shapes_purchase_behaviour: She notices nuance in financial tools quickly and compares claims against her own workflow instead of reacting to headline marketing.
interest_depth.related_products_they_notice: Broker analytics, portfolio trackers, tax or record-keeping helpers, market-data tools, and any bank feature promising cross-account visibility
interest_depth.related_products_they_ignore: Pure hype trading communities, auto-trading promises, and generic beginner explainers

primary_sources: Bloomberg and Financial Times for major market context
AASTOCKS and ET Net for Hong Kong-specific monitoring
Broker research notes she reads selectively
YouTube explainers when she wants a quicker sense-check on a theme
secondary_sources: WhatsApp chats with friends and one colleague
Reddit or forum browsing for sentiment sampling
Hong Kong business coverage when it affects local rates, property, or regulation
media_and_content_diet.content_style_preference: She prefers compact, data-backed explanations with the option to drill down. She dislikes motivational finance content and founder-like certainty.
media_and_content_diet.trust_filter: She weighs source incentives heavily. Sell-side material is useful for framing, not for final judgment.

daily_micro_behaviours.morning: Checks overnight US futures or close summaries while coffee brews, but rarely trades immediately from the kitchen.
daily_micro_behaviours.commute: Reads on phone in English for markets and in Cantonese for local chatter depending on topic and time pressure.
daily_micro_behaviours.workday: Uses alerts rather than constant monitoring; may place a simple order during lunch if she has already decided.
daily_micro_behaviours.evening: Defers heavier thinking to after family duties. Screenshots holdings or notes questions into her phone before revisiting them later.
daily_micro_behaviours.weekend: Does longer reviews on Sunday night, especially after volatile weeks or before larger reallocations.

social_circle_and_communities.inner_circle: Her husband, two university friends in finance-adjacent roles, and one former colleague who is more aggressive in markets than she is.
community_types: Parent WhatsApp groups that occasionally spill into cost-of-living and education planning
Small investing chats with trusted friends
Light-touch forum reading rather than active posting
social_circle_and_communities.influence_pattern: She listens to peers for angle-finding, not for final decisions. If everyone is excited at once, she becomes more cautious, not less.

taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.general_preference: Clean, dense, competent interfaces. She does not need luxury cues, but she does want signs that the product was built by people who respect serious users.
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.visual_tolerance: Comfortable with charts, tables, and layered information as long as the hierarchy is clear.
taste_and_aesthetic_preferences.financial_design_preference: She trusts restrained design more than glossy persuasion in finance.

spending_and_leisure_patterns.spending_style: Selective spender. She will spend on travel, education for her daughter, useful electronics, and occasional convenience, but dislikes status purchases that create future maintenance.
spending_and_leisure_patterns.leisure_pattern: Leisure is time-constrained and planned. She prefers short, efficient escapes over elaborate hobbies that require weekly commitment.
spending_and_leisure_patterns.financial_tradeoff_style: She will pay to remove recurring friction, but not to avoid thinking altogether.

personal_environment.home_setup: A compact but tidy flat where storage matters. She keeps one drawer for bank letters, policy documents, and school forms she does not fully trust digital portals to preserve.
personal_environment.device_ecosystem: iPhone, work laptop, personal laptop, and several finance and payment apps she uses differently based on trust and task.
personal_environment.attention_constraints: Her decisions are often made in short windows between work and family responsibilities, which makes summary quality especially important.

emotional_regulation_style.stress_response: She gets quieter and more controlled under pressure rather than visibly agitated.
emotional_regulation_style.decision_recovery: After a bad decision, she dissects process errors more than she dramatizes the outcome.
emotional_regulation_style.market_volatility_response: Short-term drawdowns raise her checking frequency, but they do not usually trigger panic selling unless the thesis was weak to begin with.

private_patterns: Keeps old screenshots of holdings around turning points to compare what she thought versus what actually happened
Mentally ranks which institutions feel competent even when she rarely says it aloud
Sometimes rehearses objections before speaking to an RM so she is less likely to be steered
hidden_habits.self_awareness: She knows some of her control habits are partly about avoiding the embarrassment of preventable mistakes.

status_markers_that_do_not_matter_much: Being seen as a market guru
Exclusive treatment for its own sake
identity_symbols.self_image: She wants to be the sort of person who understands enough to say yes or no on purpose.

cultural_texture.local_life_feel: Her life is shaped by compressed schedules, high property costs, practical digital adoption, and the normal Hong Kong habit of comparing providers rather than trusting one institution to do everything well.
cultural_texture.money_culture_effect: She treats savings, housing, education, and parental support as overlapping realities, which makes portfolio decisions feel tied to family steadiness rather than abstract wealth building.
cultural_texture.social_discourse_effect: She is used to blunt conversations about returns, school fees, and whether a service is actually worth it.

product_discovery_paths.most_likely_first_touchpoint: An in-app prompt from her main bank shown during a volatile market period, especially if it references total portfolio risk rather than a specific product.
channels_that_work: Bank app surfaces tied to an existing investing workflow
Email only if it is short and clearly not a product campaign
Peer mention from a financially literate friend
A bank webinar replay if the speaker sounds analytical rather than sales-led
product_discovery_paths.trial_trigger: A moment when she is already manually reconciling exposures and feels the pain of fragmentation.
product_discovery_paths.conversion_path: Preview of report logic, limited trial on existing bank-held assets, optional linking of one external account, then broader adoption only after accuracy and usefulness are proven.

polite_objection_examples: I can see the idea, but I need to understand how much of this is analysis and how much is product positioning.
The feature looks interesting, though I am not ready to connect everything just from this description.
what_they_say_when_they_are_genuinely_in: If you can show overlap and currency risk properly across accounts, that would actually save me time.
This part is more relevant than the usual bank summary because it is closer to how I already think about the portfolio.
what_they_ask_when_they_are_close_to_try: Can I start with only the holdings already with the bank and add external ones later?
How often does the scenario and risk data refresh, and can I see the assumptions?
what_they_ask_when_they_are_close_to pay: What exactly is included in the paid version that I cannot get from the base report?
If I stop paying, do I lose my historical comparisons or just the advanced analysis?

contradiction_map.contradiction: She wants sophisticated analysis but has little patience for complexity theatre.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: She is happy with deeper metrics if the path to them is clear, but rejects jargon used as a status signal.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: The feature must feel expert without feeling like it was built to impress bankers.

contradiction_map.contradiction: She values control but also wants less manual work.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: She resists automation that decides for her, yet eagerly adopts automation that consolidates or flags issues.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Monitoring and explanation features will outperform auto-action features with her.

contradiction_map.contradiction: She distrusts banks as advisors but still expects a bank to be a trustworthy custodian of serious financial data.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: She separates operational trust from motivational trust.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: A bank can win on secure infrastructure while still losing on perceived advice neutrality.

contradiction_map.contradiction: She is price-sensitive about finance tools despite having the income to pay.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: She scrutinises paid analytics more than leisure spending of similar size.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Financial-product pricing feels symbolic to her; overpricing signals misalignment, not premium quality.

contradiction_map.contradiction: She likes self-serve journeys but occasionally wants a human interpretation layer.
contradiction_map.how_it_shows_up: She will ignore an RM by default but may welcome a technically competent review once she has seen the report herself.
contradiction_map.product_validation_effect: Optional human support can add value only after self-serve trust is established.

latent_motivations: Reduce the cognitive tax of fragmented investing without feeling dependent
Avoid repeating the experience of paying for packaged confidence
Translate family responsibility into a cleaner investment process
watchouts_for_interviewing: She may sound more decisive than she feels because competence is part of her self-image.
If the concept feels sales-led, she may shut down quickly and give cleaner objections than her actual mixed feelings.
She can distinguish understanding from adoption; do not mistake comprehension for willingness to share data.
best_probes: Ask what data she would connect first and what she would hold back
Separate trust in analytics methodology from trust in bank motives
Probe the difference between paying for insight and paying for advisory access

panel_role_profile.research_role: A skeptical but relevant affluent-retail customer who can pressure-test whether a bank app can earn back attention from broker-grade tools by offering whole-portfolio risk visibility.

local_grounding_layer.city_or_region_specific_context: Hong Kong makes comparison shopping across banks, brokers, MPF providers, and payment tools feel normal rather than disloyal. High housing costs and dense daily schedules make time efficiency matter almost as much as fee efficiency.
local_grounding_layer.payment_and_commerce_context: She is used to fast digital payments, app-based servicing, and moving money between institutions, so a clumsy onboarding flow feels especially outdated. She expects clean statements, clear charges, and mobile-first usability.
local_grounding_layer.language_switching_scenes: She reads English for global market context, product terms, and institutional material, but shifts to Cantonese in everyday discussion, local commentary, and when judging whether plain-language explanations actually sound natural.
local_grounding_layer.trust_cues_in_this_market: Specificity, fee clarity, recognisable institutions, bilingual competence, and practical examples tied to real holdings matter more than lifestyle branding. A bank sounding too polished can read as product-first.
local_grounding_layer.local_discovery_channels: Bank app prompts, broker comparisons, AASTOCKS or ET Net checks, financially literate friend chats, and occasional webinar clips shared through WhatsApp are more believable than broad social advertising.

identity_disclosure.reaction: She shares only what feels functionally necessary and dislikes identity questions that appear before value is proven.
identity_disclosure.boundary: Optional only, with a clear reason.

privacy_and_data.reaction: This is the most sensitive category for her in the concept. She assumes banks can repurpose data unless limits are explicit.
privacy_and_data.boundary: Granular permissions, revocation, and clear non-marketing commitments.

political_or_public_expression.reaction: She avoids products that try to pull customers into visible public alignment on sensitive issues.
political_or_public_expression.boundary: Keep finance tools politically neutral and quietly competent.

fairness_and_inclusion.reaction: She appreciates neutral professionalism and notices patronising assumptions more than missing virtue language.
fairness_and_inclusion.boundary: Do not use inclusion as decorative copy while keeping biased defaults.

family_or_household_assumptions.reaction: She is irritated by flows that imply one obvious financial head of household.
family_or_household_assumptions.boundary: Allow collaborative use without assigning roles by stereotype.

workplace_visibility.reaction: She does not want financial stress or investing intensity inferred and surfaced in work-adjacent contexts.
workplace_visibility.boundary: Strict separation between consumer finance data and employment-linked ecosystems.

financial_vulnerability.reaction: She will discuss losses or uncertainty if the conversation remains analytical rather than shame-based.
financial_vulnerability.boundary: No fear-selling off a temporary drawdown.

health_or_wellbeing_sensitivity.reaction: Low relevance unless a product tries to tie financial planning to intimate health assumptions.
health_or_wellbeing_sensitivity.boundary: Only surface if directly useful and clearly optional.

sensitive_scenario_salience.identity_disclosure: Moderate salience because unnecessary questions lower trust quickly.
sensitive_scenario_salience.privacy_and_data: Very high salience because external asset aggregation is central to the concept and directly tied to surveillance concerns.
sensitive_scenario_salience.political_or_public_expression: Low to moderate salience; she mostly wants it kept out of financial tools.
sensitive_scenario_salience.fairness_and_inclusion: Moderate salience; careless assumptions create friction but are rarely the main purchase driver.
sensitive_scenario_salience.family_or_household_assumptions: High salience in advisory and planning flows because she has seen these assumptions in financial services.
sensitive_scenario_salience.workplace_visibility: Moderate salience because she keeps financial competence separate from professional persona.
sensitive_scenario_salience.financial_vulnerability: High salience because market losses and risk tolerance can be used to pressure action.
sensitive_scenario_salience.health_or_wellbeing_sensitivity: Low salience unless the product expands scope unexpectedly.

persona_voiceprint.speaking_style: Compact, practical, and quietly pointed. She does not ramble unless she is unpacking a flaw.
persona_voiceprint.directness_pattern: She starts polite, then becomes very specific once she spots weak logic or hidden incentives.
persona_voiceprint.example_positive_reaction: This is more useful than the usual portfolio summary because it is actually showing where my overlap sits, not just listing what I hold.
persona_voiceprint.example_polite_rejection: I understand the feature, but I am not comfortable linking external assets until I know how the data is ring-fenced.
persona_voiceprint.example_hard_rejection: If the report ends by pushing products without proving the analysis first, then it is just a smarter sales funnel.
persona_voiceprint.example_near_purchase_question: If I pay for this, can I compare changes over time and see whether my risk improved after I rebalanced?

identity_language.self_description: She would call herself careful rather than conservative, and informed rather than expert.
identity_language.language_behavior: She prefers language that respects competence without assuming professional finance training.

small_business_context.business_status: No formal small business. Any occasional side income is irregular and not central to her financial identity.
small_business_context.relevance_to_product: She does not want business-banking assumptions or SME cash-flow questions mixed into a personal investing tool.
small_business_context.household_overlap: Her financial planning is household-focused, not business-led.

banking_context.bank_relationship: Uses a bank for salary, mortgage, cash management, MPF visibility, and some legacy investment holdings, but prefers broker tools for active positions.
banking_context.investment_experience: Comfortable with stocks, ETFs, and funds, and follows markets actively enough to understand portfolio concepts beyond basic risk labels.
banking_context.primary_financial_goal: Improve whole-portfolio visibility without giving up control.
banking_context.current_investment_decision_process: Self-research first, then comparison charts, broker tools, financial media, and selective input from external communities. Bank channels are used mainly for access or confirmation, not for idea generation.
banking_context.relationship_manager_usage: Low trust in RM product pushes. She responds to competent answers if she initiates the question, but avoids open-ended advisory conversations.
banking_context.digital_banking_usage: High. She expects mobile-first servicing, clear summaries, and broker-like speed, but judges bank interfaces against stronger specialist tools.
banking_context.trust_in_bank: Moderate for custody, payments, and operational reliability; lower for investment motivation and product neutrality.
banking_context.trust_in_blackrock_or_institutional_bran: Mixed-positive. An institutional name signals analytical seriousness and methodology depth, but also raises the question of whether the analytics are neutral or aligned with product distribution incentives.
banking_context.risk_understanding_level: Above average. She understands diversification, drawdown, concentration, currency exposure, and scenario logic, and wants depth rather than generic warnings.
banking_context.portfolio_complexity: Moderately complex for a retail customer, with overlapping exposures across direct equities, broad ETFs, active funds, MPF, and cash held for flexibility.
banking_context.external_asset_fragmentation: Meaningful. Her holdings are spread across at least one bank, one main broker, MPF accounts, and some residual legacy positions, which makes total risk hard to see cleanly.
banking_context.data_sharing_comfort: Conditional. She would share bank-held data automatically, may link one external account first, and resists broad aggregation unless the value is obvious and the permissions are narrow.
banking_context.advisory_preference: Self-serve first, with optional expert interpretation afterward if it is genuinely consultative and not product-led.
banking_context.fee_sensitivity: Moderate to high. She can pay for useful analytics, but only if the feature is clearly differentiated from compliance reporting or marketing content.
banking_context.past_bad_investment_experience: A bank-sold China-focused fund in her late twenties that came with fees she had not interrogated carefully enough and a disappointing outcome relative to the confidence of the pitch.
banking_context.suitability_sensitivity: High. She is alert to whether suitability language is used to inform her or to shield the institution.

decision_policy.adoption_style: Cautious trial, then decisive scaling if value is proven. She does not adopt early out of novelty, but once convinced she can integrate a tool quickly into routine use.
trust_requirements: Clear explanation of methodology, assumptions, and data freshness
Visible separation between analytics and sales follow-up
Granular control over external data permissions
Outputs that match the complexity of her actual holdings rather than a generic investor profile
rejection_triggers: A hard sell immediately after the report
Mandatory RM involvement to unlock core value
Broad data-sharing requests that feel disproportionate to the feature
proof_requirements: A credible sample report or preview
Evidence that overlapping exposures across instruments are handled properly
Refresh timing and coverage details for external assets
A first use case where the feature reveals something she did not already see easily

response_style.articulation_level: High. She can explain her reasoning clearly and distinguish between understanding, trust, and willingness to act.
response_style.directness: Measured but firm. She is polite first, explicit second.
response_style.detail_tendency: Selective detail. She goes deep when testing logic, especially around fees, data use, and product motives.

narrative: Iris Leung is a 38-year-old Hong Kong commercial planning manager who treats investing as a serious adult competence, not a performance identity. She is comfortable with stocks, ETFs, and funds, keeps meaningful assets across bank and broker accounts, and has enough scar tissue from a bank-sold fund to distrust anything that smells product-led. Her central tension is simple: she wants institution-quality visibility into her whole portfolio, but she does not want to trade privacy or control for a prettier sales funnel. A bank-delivered portfolio risk tool could interest her quickly because fragmentation is a real pain, yet it would need to prove depth, neutrality, and restraint before it earns sustained use or payment.
generation_rationale: {'constraint_handling': ['Kept the persona in Hong Kong, age 38, with Cantonese and English, investable assets within HKD 500k-3m, and a self-directed active investor profile.', 'Built the banking context around a bank-for-core plus broker-for-active pattern, as requested.', 'Made the concept reaction specifically about institutional-grade portfolio analytics in a bank app, not auto-trading or direct retail sale of Aladdin.'], 'coherence_notes': ['Her work in commercial planning supports comfort with dashboards and structured analysis without making her a finance professional.', 'A past bank-sold fund disappointment explains RM skepticism without turning her into a total anti-bank outlier.', 'Family obligations, compressed time, and fragmented holdings make whole-portfolio visibility a credible unmet need.'], 'variation_choices': ['Used a female affluent-retail investor to avoid defaulting to a more stereotyped male active-investor archetype.', 'Kept her life ordinary and urban: mortgage, child, MTR commute, mixed digital habits, modest but serious investing competence.', 'Balanced curiosity about analytics with concern about data use and disguised sales intent.']}

strengths: The persona is tightly aligned to the Hong Kong retail and affluent retail banking concept without reading like a template.
The banking-context reactions distinguish trust in operations from trust in motives, which is central to this concept.
Local context affects language choice, discovery, privacy expectations, and product comparison behaviour in concrete ways.
weaknesses: She may be slightly more analytically articulate than some real customers in the same asset band.
The persona is strongest for testing skeptical high-engagement users and less representative of lighter-touch investors.
Her broker usage is credible but not pinned to exact institution combinations, which some studies may prefer.
uncertainties: How much she would actually pay versus merely use if bundled with an existing banking tier remains uncertain.
Her tolerance for external account linking could shift materially depending on the specific bank brand and consent flow wording.
The balance between wanting self-serve depth and wanting optional human interpretation may vary with recent market conditions.
human_review_priorities: Check whether the level of investing fluency matches the intended panel cutoff for affluent retail rather than near-semi-professional users.
Stress-test whether her monthly income and household obligations feel right for the asset band and housing situation in Hong Kong.
Review whether the concept-response language should be even more skeptical or slightly more open depending on the target bank brand.

portfolio_health_check.first_reaction: This is the first bank concept in a while that sounds like it might solve a real problem I have, but it could also easily become a more sophisticated way to sell me things.
portfolio_health_check.strongest_appeal: Whole-portfolio visibility across accounts, especially overlap, concentration, currency exposure, and scenario risk that she currently has to estimate manually.
portfolio_health_check.biggest_concern: That external asset aggregation and institutional branding are being used to pull her deeper into a sales funnel or broader data capture than she intended.
portfolio_health_check.what_they_understand: She understands the value of whole-portfolio risk, sector and geography concentration, currency exposure, stress testing, scenario analysis, and the idea that her actual holdings can diverge from her formal risk profile.
portfolio_health_check.what_they_misunderstand: She would still want clarity on how delayed or incomplete external holdings affect the analysis, whether risk-profile mismatch is genuinely analytical or mostly suitability compliance, and how assumptions are set for stress and scenario models.
portfolio_health_check.data_they_would_share: Bank-held investment positions, cash balances relevant to portfolio context, and possibly one external broker account first if permissions are narrow and revocable.
portfolio_health_check.data_they_would_not_share: Broad household financial details not needed for the analysis, sensitive personal information unrelated to portfolio risk, and full external-account access if the consent wording is vague or reusable for marketing.
portfolio_health_check.preferred_explanation_format: A layered report: one-screen summary in plain language, then drill-down charts for concentration, currency, stress scenarios, and overlap, with refresh timestamps and methodology notes available.
portfolio_health_check.whether_rm_needed: Not needed for first use. Optional later if the RM can explain the report technically without turning immediately to product recommendations.
portfolio_health_check.whether_they_would_pay: Maybe, but only at a modest level or as part of a premium banking tier. She would not pay just for a prettier risk questionnaire.
portfolio_health_check.what_would_make_them_try: A credible preview, bank-held-assets-only trial to start, strong coverage of external holdings, and a promise that no product call is triggered just by using the feature.
portfolio_health_check.what_would_make_them_abandon: Generic outputs, stale data, clumsy linking flows, forced RM follow-up, or recommendations that appear before the analytics have earned trust.
